title: Ensure only MFA enabled identities can access privileged  Virtual Machine

input_row.title: Ensure only MFA enabled identities can access privileged  Virtual Machine
input_row.description: Verify identities without MFA that can log in to a privileged virtual machine using separate login credentials. An adversary can leverage the access to move laterally and perform actions with the virtual machine's managed identity. Make sure the virtual machine only has necessary permissions, and revoke the admin-level permissions according to the principle of least privilege.
input_row.rationale: Integrating multi-factor authentication (MFA) as part of the organizational policy can greatly reduce the risk of an identity gaining control of valid credentials that may be used for additional tactics such as initial access, lateral movement, and collecting information. MFA can also be used to restrict access to cloud resources and APIs. An Adversary may log into accessible cloud services within a compromised environment using Valid Accounts that are synchronized to move laterally and perform actions with the virtual machine's managed identity. The adversary may then perform management actions or access cloud-hosted resources as the logged-on managed identity.
input_row.audit: Audit from Azure Portal 1. Log in to the Azure portal. 2. Select the Subscription, then click on Access control (IAM). 3. Click Role : All and click All to display the drop-down menu. 4. Type Virtual Machine Administrator Login and select Virtual Machine Administrator Login. 5. Review the list of identities that have been assigned the Virtual Machine Administrator Login role. 6. Go to Microsoft Entra ID. 7. For Per-user MFA: a) Under Manage, click Users. b) Click Per-user MFA. c) Ensure that none of the identities assigned the Virtual Machine Administrator Login role from step 4 have Status set to disabled. 8. For Conditional Access: a) Under Manage, click Security. b) Under Protect, click Conditional Access. c) Ensure that none of the identities assigned the Virtual Machine Administrator Login role from step 4 are exempt from a Conditional Access policy requiring MFA for all users.
input_row.remediation: Remediate from Azure Portal 1. Log in to the Azure portal. 2. This can be remediated by enabling MFA for user, Removing user access or Reducing access of managed identities attached to virtual machines. Case I : Enable MFA for users having access on virtual machines. 1. Go to Microsoft Entra ID. 2. For Per-user MFA: 1. Under Manage, click Users. 2. Click Per-user MFA. 3. For each user requiring remediation, check the box next to their name. 4. Click Enable MFA. 5. Click Enable. 3. For Conditional Access: 1. Under Manage, click Security. 2. Under Protect, click Conditional Access. 3. Update the Conditional Access policy requiring MFA for all users, removing each user requiring remediation from the Exclude list. Case II : Removing user access on a virtual machine. 1. Select the Subscription, then click on Access control (IAM). 2. Select Role assignments and search for Virtual Machine Administrator Login or Virtual Machine User Login or any role that provides access to log into virtual machines. 3. Click on Role Name, Select Assignments, and remove identities with no MFA configured. Case III : Reducing access of managed identities attached to virtual machines. 1. Select the Subscription, then click on Access control (IAM). 2. Select Role Assignments from the top menu and apply filters on Assignment type as Privileged administrator roles and Type as Virtual Machines. 3. Click on Role Name, Select Assignments, and remove identities access make sure this follows the least privileges principal.
input_row.impact: This recommendation requires the Entra ID P2 license to implement. Ensure that identities provisioned to a virtual machine utilize an RBAC/ABAC group and are allocated a role using Azure PIM, and that the role settings require MFA or use another third-party PAM solution for accessing virtual machines.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. **Access AWS Management Console**: 
   - Navigate to the AWS Management Console and log in with your credentials.
   
2. **Check IAM Roles for EC2 Instances**:
   - Go to the EC2 Dashboard.
   - Select "Instances" and find the instances that you need to review.
   - For each instance, check the "IAM role" associated with it by selecting the instance and looking at its description.
   
3. **Review IAM Role Permissions**:
   - Go to the IAM Dashboard.
   - Select "Roles" and look for the role(s) assigned to the EC2 instance.
   - Review the attached policies to identify permissions related to access control and management actions.

4. **Check for MFA on Users with These Roles**:
   - Navigate to IAM in the AWS Management Console. 
   - Select "Users" and find the users who have the appropriate IAM role that provides access to the EC2 instance.
   - Click on each user to check their "Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)" device status.

5. **Confirm MFA Status**:
   - Ensure that MFA is enabled for all identified users with access to the EC2 instance.
   - If MFA is not enabled, take note of such users as they may represent a security risk.
   
6. **Expected Secure Configuration**:
   - All IAM users with access to the EC2 instance should have MFA enabled to minimize the risk of unauthorized access.

7. **Confirm Compliance**:
   - Document your findings. Compliance can be confirmed when all users with access have MFA enabled. If any user lacks MFA, immediate remedial actions should be taken, such as enabling MFA for those users.

The detailed steps above ensure a thorough manual audit of MFA settings for users with access to privileged EC2 instances.
